messages.text:
The convex hull of a set Q of points is the smallest convex  polygon P for which each point in Q is either on the boundary of P or in its interior. Given a set of n two-dimensional points i.e. (x, y) points.  Implement Graham's Scan algorithm in C++ to find the convex hull of the given set of points. Take the set of points as input by clicking the mouse on the  screen, compute the convex hull using  Graham's Scan algorithm and show  it step by step on the screen.  If a new point is added to the given set of points, the convex hull needs to be changed (or recomputed).
with GUI and i am using vscode as IDE and macbook pro 2019 as device

code_blocks:
main.cpp
│
├── GUI Layer (SFML)
│      ├── Mouse click → add point
│      ├── Draw points
│      └── Draw hull edges
│
├── Geometry Engine
│      ├── orientation()
│      ├── distance()
│      └── grahamScan()
│
└── Visualization
       └── step-by-step hull construction

Mouse Click
   ↓
Point added to vector
   ↓
Recompute Convex Hull
   ↓
Animate Graham Scan
   ↓
Render Hull

Left Mouse Click → Add Point
                  ↓
Recompute Hull
                  ↓
Draw Points (White)
Draw Hull (Green)

Click 5 points
        ↓
Algorithm sorts by polar angle
        ↓
Stack operations build hull
        ↓
Polygon rendered

push point
draw line
wait
if pop occurs
draw removal
wait

Sorting → O(n log n)
Scan    → O(n)

Total   → O(n log n)

14 warnings and 20 errors generated.

Your code is fine. The failure is purely a compiler flag issue.
SFML you installed requires C++17, but your compile command is using the default C++ standard (C++98).

That’s why you see errors like:

Enable C++17 or newer

std::optional not found

std::filesystem not found

Those are C++17 features.

Fix (One-Line Change)

Add the C++17 flag.

Bash
g++ -std=c++17 main.cpp -o hull \
-I/opt/homebrew/include \
-L/opt/homebrew/lib \
-lsfml-graphics -lsfml-window -lsfml-system

Run:

Bash
./hull
If You Still Get Errors (Mac Quirk)

Sometimes Mac installs SFML in /usr/local instead of /opt/homebrew.

Check where it actually exists:

Bash
ls /usr/local/include/SFML

If it exists there, compile like this:

Bash
g++ -std=c++17 main.cpp -o hull \
-I/usr/local/include \
-L/usr/local/lib \
-lsfml-graphics -lsfml-window -lsfml-system
Clean Professional Compile Command (Recommended)

Use this:

Bash
g++ main.cpp -std=c++17 -lsfml-graphics -lsfml-window -lsfml-system -o hull

SFML on Mac usually auto-links.

How the Program Should Work After Fix

When it runs:

Window opens

Left click → point appears

Add several points

Convex hull polygon appears

Add another point → hull recomputes automatically

Quick Demo Interaction

Click points like:

(100,100)
(200,300)
(400,200)
(350,500)

Program will draw:

*--------*
 \      /
  \    /
   *--*
Important Tip (For Your Assignment Demo)

Explain this in viva:

Why Graham Scan works

Key invariant:

While turn is not counterclockwise
      remove last hull point

Orientation formula:

(q.y - p.y)*(r.x - q.x) -
(q.x - p.x)*(r.y - q.y)

Time complexity:

O(n log n)

Sorting dominates.

If you want, I can also give you

1️⃣ A much cleaner version with step-by-step animation
2️⃣ Better GUI (points numbered + hull order)
3️⃣ Professional project folder for VSCode
4️⃣ Screenshot-ready visualization for submission

Just say “give the animated Graham Scan version”. It looks 10× more impressive in project demos.
code_blocks:
While turn is not counterclockwise
      remove last hull point

messages.text: why it is showing vector is not a template

code_blocks:
click → add all points
press RUN → show Graham Scan step-by-step

Left Click  → Add points
Press R     → Run Graham Scan

white dots  = input points
green lines = step-by-step hull
red lines   = final convex hull
